ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish with fish and spices — biryani type; mapped to composite rice-based dish with fish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for fish biryani; A0FJP (rice-based composite dish) used as closest defensible parent anchor. Fish biryani is a composite dish not individually enumerated in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fish biryani as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Fish Biryani in Singapore is approximately 525 g, comprising a generous mound of spiced basmati rice layered with marinated fish, fried onions, and aromatic herbs, typically served with raita or curry sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for biryani-type rice dishes with fish. Values were cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for 'Rice, white, long-grain, cooked' (ID 169756) and 'Fish, mixed species, cooked' as component anchors. Composite dish values accepted at medium confidence. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1 as detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite dish is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Basmati rice, white, boiled (GI ~58)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fish Biryani as a composite dish. GI of 58 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: basmati white rice, boiled, which has a published GI of approximately 58 (medium) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 16.36 × 525/100) / 100 = (58 × 85.89) / 100 ≈ 49.8 g GL per 525 g plate. The presence of fish protein, yogurt, and spices (turmeric, cinnamon) may modestly reduce the effective GI of the composite dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 218 mg/100g; a standard 525 g plate delivers approximately 1145 mg sodium, representing 50–57% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 300–350 g, avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as papadum or pickled achar, and request minimal ghee preparation to reduce both sodium load and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content of 16.4 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100g) and a medium GI of approximately 58 from basmati rice creates a meaningful glycaemic load of ~50 g GL per standard plate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 150–200 g cooked, increase the fish component for protein-to-carbohydrate ratio improvement, and note that turmeric and cinnamon in the spice blend have evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties that may modestly attenuate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.82 g/100g; fish provides omega-3 fatty acids (particularly if King Mackerel or Pomfret is used); trans fat is present at a low level of 0.09 g/100g; cholesterol is 21.82 mg/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish Biryani is broadly suitable for cardiovascular disease management when prepared with oily fish (mackerel, pomfret) for omega-3 benefit, minimal ghee, and without high-sodium accompaniments; individuals should monitor total sodium intake across the meal occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric/curcumin, ginger, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom) and potential omega-3 content from oily fish varieties; dum-cooking avoids AGE-generating deep-frying.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish Biryani is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor their response to tomatoes and chilli, and should request caramelised rather than deep-fried onions to minimise AGE intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Biryani (also spelled Fish Briyani or Fish Biriyani) is a fragrant, layered rice dish popular across Singapore, Malaysia, and South Asia. It is prepared using the dum cooking method: marinated fish fillets (commonly King Mackerel, Pomfret, or Dory) are layered with partially cooked basmati rice, fried onions, fresh mint, coriander, and a complex spice blend including turmeric, cumin, coriander, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, and bay leaves, then sealed and slow-cooked together so the flavours meld. The marinade typically includes yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, chilli powder, and lime juice, which tenderises the fish and imparts a tangy depth. The finished dish has a fluffy, aromatic rice texture with moist, spiced fish pieces; the colour ranges from golden-yellow to orange from turmeric and saffron or food colouring. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 155 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 6.4 g fat (1.8 g saturated), 16.4 g carbohydrate, 1.1 g dietary fibre, and 218 mg sodium. The medium glycaemic index (~58, basmati rice basis) and moderate sodium content make portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The anti-inflammatory spice profile (curcumin, gingerol, cinnamaldehyde) and potential omega-3 content from oily fish varieties make it a nutritionally positive choice for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular health when prepared with minimal ghee. In Singapore, Fish Biryani is a staple at Indian-Muslim (Mamak) restaurants and hawker stalls, typically served with raita (yogurt-cucumber condiment) or a thin fish curry gravy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish biryani?
Is fish briyani high in sodium?
What fish is used in fish biryani Singapore?
Fish biryani vs chicken biryani calories
Is fish biryani good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for biryani-type dish entries and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central component anchors. The composite dish nature and variability in fish species and ghee quantity introduce moderate uncertainty. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (basmati rice, GI ~58) rather than a direct measurement of the composite dish. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient profile for this composite fish biryani dish are not available as a single composite entry in HPB Foodo Singapore or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. trans_fat_g of 0.09 g/100g is a low but non-zero value from the input source; retained as reported. 3. Portion size standardised to 525 g (mean of three input portion values: 550, 500, 550 g) as the default serving for GL calculation. Input contained three duplicate portion entries all labelled '1 plate' with is_default=true; consolidated to single default portion of 525 g. 4. Ethnic tags in input ('Noodle', 'Fried Rice', 'Roti Prata', 'Dosa') were clearly erroneous for this dish and have been corrected. 5. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on basmati white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for fish biryani as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 6. All core macros (protein_g 8.18, fat_g 6.36, carbohydrate_g 16.36) are non-zero and do not require zero-macro justification. 7. foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent': A0FJP is the closest parent category in FoodEx2 for a composite rice-based dish with fish; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for fish biryani as a named dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON, multilingual names added, ontology anchors set, health context expanded to include cardiovascular disease, GI estimated from basmati rice analogue.
Iteration 2: Corrected nutrient_source.title, verified aliases consistency, confirmed all -1 sentinel values documented in notes.

Iteration 3: Fixed g5_ontology_honesty hard gate failure — changed foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' (not an allowed value) to 'closest_parent' (a valid allowed value per scoring model). Updated foodex2_gap_note and notes field to document this correction explicitly.
